teaching_arc.entry_point: Start from a realistic robot-selection decision instead of starting with vocabulary in isolation.
teaching_arc.progression: Move through the specification terms students need first, then compare manipulator families by workspace and use case.
teaching_arc.destination: Students should finish able to explain both what the main specs mean and which manipulator type fits a given task.

audience_level: undergraduate industrial robotics students preparing for labs, robot selection, and basic programming decisions
topic_goal: Teach students to read robot specifications like engineers and to match manipulator families to real task constraints.
topic_takeaways: Students should distinguish key datasheet terms such as payload, reach, workspace, accuracy, and repeatability.
Students should understand how manipulator geometry changes workspace and best-fit applications.
Students should be able to justify a robot choice using task, workspace, and performance trade-offs.
style_notes: Keep the tone practical and shop-floor oriented.
Translate definitions into robot-cell decisions quickly.
Prefer concrete industrial examples over abstract theory when possible.
Make the slide-to-slide flow feel like the next question an engineer asks while reading a datasheet.
Use clear contrast wording when comparing reach, workspace, accuracy, and manipulator families.
Bring in spatial props only when they materially improve intuition.
transition_style: Treat the lesson like a robot-selection walkthrough: define the metric, remove a likely confusion, then use it to compare manipulator options.

slides.narration_seed: Open the lesson by explaining that this topic has two parts: robot characteristics from datasheets, and manipulator types used in industry.
must_cover: This lesson has two parts: characteristics and manipulator types.
Students should leave able to predict what a robot is good at.
must_say: This topic is really about learning how to predict what a robot will be good at before you ever see it move.
slides.transition_from_previous: Open by telling students they are about to learn how engineers read robot specifications and compare manipulator families.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to justify why specification literacy matters before introducing the actual metrics.
likely_student_confusion: Students often think robot choice is mostly about brand or payload, instead of a combination of task, workspace, accuracy, and speed.
slides.explanation_style: Frame the lesson as a real robot-selection conversation, not a list of datasheet terms.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-selection meeting where someone needs to justify a purchase to a team.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should feel that every specification in this lesson answers a practical design question.
slides.scene_hint: Use a simplified robot silhouette and workspace volume so students can see how geometry shapes where the robot can work.

slide_ref.source_title: Why do we learn robot characteristics?

slides.narration_seed: Make this practical and industrial, not academic.
must_cover: Because real robots are chosen from datasheets (specs decide the choice)
Wrong choice = slow cell, poor accuracy, poor repeatability, or safety issues
You should be able to answer: “Is this robot suitable for the job?”
Real robot selection is based on datasheets and specifications.
Wrong selection can create slow, inaccurate, or unsafe cells.
must_say: Robot characteristics are the language engineers use to avoid buying the wrong robot for the job.
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from the course framing into the practical reason this vocabulary matters in industry.
slides.transition_to_next: Once the stakes are clear, the next slide can give students a quick map of the lesson.
likely_student_confusion: Students may think characteristics are only for exams, not for real selection and integration work.
slides.explanation_style: Keep this grounded in industrial consequences such as wrong robot sizing, wasted money, or unsafe cell layouts.
slides.story_hint: Keep Why do we learn robot characteristics? connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should believe this vocabulary will help them make better engineering decisions.

slides.narration_seed: We’ll keep each concept short. I’ll give you the practical meaning and one example.
must_cover: Part A: Characteristics (datasheet vocabulary you must understand)
Part B: Manipulator types (workspace shapes + best use cases)
Quick practice: pick a robot type for a task and justify your choice
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from Why do we learn robot characteristics? into Today’s route so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Robot selection = 5 quick questions so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Use Today’s route to orient students, preview the route, and tell them what to listen for next.
slides.story_hint: Keep Today’s route connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how Today’s route sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slides.narration_seed: Present the five questions as a fast mental checklist.
must_cover: 1) What task? (weld, pick, cut, paint, assemble)
2) What payload? (tool + part + cables + brackets)
3) What reach/workspace do we need (and any obstacles)?
Task
Payload
Reach and workspace
Speed
Accuracy and repeatability
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Robot selection = 5 quick questions to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Robot selection = 5 quick questions is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Robot selection = 5 quick questions in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Robot selection = 5 quick questions connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Robot selection = 5 quick questions accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Introduce DoF as practical freedom of motion and flexibility.
must_cover: DoF: the number of different ways in which the robot can move
Typically equal to the number of actuators (how many independent joints you can command)
Different tasks require different DoF (more DoF = more flexibility, but more complexity/cost)
DoF is the number of independent ways the robot can move.
More DoF usually means more flexibility but more complexity.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Why 6 DoF is the ‘standard’ in 3D so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students may count axes without connecting them to the practical freedom the robot gains.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Degrees of Freedom (DoF) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep Degrees of Freedom (DoF) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Degrees of Freedom (DoF) clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Explain the relation between position plus orientation in 3D.
must_cover: In 3D you often need: position (x,y,z) + orientation (roll,pitch,yaw)
6 DoF are needed for arbitrary tasks in three dimensional space
That’s why most industrial arms are 6-axis articulated manipulators
Position is x, y, z.
Orientation is roll, pitch, yaw.
That is why 6-axis articulated arms are common.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Why 6 DoF is the ‘standard’ in 3D to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Why 6 DoF is the ‘standard’ in 3D is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
likely_student_confusion: Students may count axes without connecting them to the practical freedom the robot gains.
slides.explanation_style: Define Why 6 DoF is the ‘standard’ in 3D in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Why 6 DoF is the ‘standard’ in 3D connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Why 6 DoF is the ‘standard’ in 3D accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Not every task needs 6. If you’re working on a flat surface with vertical insertion, 4 DoF can be enough—SCARA is a good example.
must_cover: For tasks not requiring rotation of the tool about the direction it is pointing (painting, welding), 5 DoF are suitable
For tasks involving moving objects in a plane (electronics assembly), 4 DoF is typically suitable
6 DoF is most useful when approach angles and orientation constraints are complex (tight spaces, varied tool angles)
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from Why 6 DoF is the ‘standard’ in 3D into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
likely_student_confusion: Students may count axes without connecting them to the practical freedom the robot gains.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on When 4 or 5 DoF is enough explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of When 4 or 5 DoF is enough and know which side fits which situation.

slides.narration_seed: This is a good mental shortcut: SCARA for fast assembly, articulated for general 3D, Cartesian for straight-line precision in a box-shaped space.
must_cover: SCARA: fast planar work + vertical insertion (often 4 DoF)
Articulated arm: flexible 3D motion (usually 6 DoF)
Gantry/Cartesian: precise XYZ motion (often 3 DoF for positioning)
slides.transition_from_previous: Use DoF examples (easy) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once DoF examples (easy) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
likely_student_confusion: Students may count axes without connecting them to the practical freedom the robot gains.
slides.explanation_style: Define DoF examples (easy) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep DoF examples (easy) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain DoF examples (easy) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Make payload practical by including the full EOAT, not just the part.
must_cover: Payload: the maximum weight that a robot can carry while performing with the quoted specifications
Payload is not just the part — include tool, cables, brackets, and EOAT hardware
Payload capability changes with reach and speed (dynamic loads matter)
Payload includes tool, part, cables, and brackets.
Effective payload depends on reach and speed.
must_say: Payload is everything the robot wrist has to carry, not just the part.
Payload is not just the part; it is everything the wrist has to carry.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Payload (what it really means) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Payload (what it really means) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
likely_student_confusion: Students often count only the part and forget the tool, bracket, and cables.
slides.explanation_style: Define Payload (what it really means) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Payload (what it really means) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.
slides.scene_hint: Briefly show a gripper, part, bracket, and cable bundle accumulating at the wrist to make total payload concrete.

slides.narration_seed: Many real failures happen because the EOAT is heavier than expected. In real applications, a margin saves you from overheating motors and losing accuracy.
must_cover: Example: part = 2 kg, gripper = 3 kg, bracket = 1 kg → payload = 6 kg
If you forget cables and brackets, you under-design the cell
Rule: add a safety margin (to reduce overheating, vibration, and wear)
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
must_say: Payload is everything the robot wrist has to carry, not just the part.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Payload quick practice and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.
likely_student_confusion: Students often count only the part and forget the tool, bracket, and cables.
slides.explanation_style: Turn Payload quick practice into a guided decision: pause for thought, then reveal the reasoning step by step.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to answer a similar Payload quick practice question on their own and explain the reasoning.
slides.scene_hint: Briefly show the tool, part, and mounting hardware together at the wrist so the full payload is visually obvious.

slides.narration_seed: Make the difference between reach and workspace very clear using the visual.
must_cover: Reach: the maximum distance the robot can extend from the base
Workspace: all the possible positions in space that a robot can place its end effector
Workspace is limited by joint/link lengths, joint limits, and obstacles in the cell
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
Reach is max extension from the base.
Workspace is the full region where the end effector can operate.
Workspace matters more for cell planning.
must_say: Reach is one maximum distance. Workspace is the full usable region.
Reach is one maximum distance; workspace is the full region where the robot can actually operate.
slides.transition_from_previous: Shift from single specifications to the spatial idea students confuse most often during robot selection.
slides.transition_to_next: After separating reach from workspace, keep the spatial intuition going by comparing common workspace shapes.
likely_student_confusion: Students often use reach and workspace as if they mean the same thing.
Students may treat the farthest point as if it describes the whole usable operating region.
Students may think the farthest point automatically describes every usable point the robot can access.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the one-number reach idea with the full 3D operating region students must design around.
slides.story_hint: Tell students about a cell that fit the reach spec on paper but still failed because the usable workspace was wrong.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember that reach is a maximum distance, while workspace is the whole usable region.
slides.scene_hint: Show a robot arm extending to one farthest point, then reveal the full operating volume so reach and workspace are visually separated.

slides.narration_seed: Give fast intuition and connect shape to robot type.
must_cover: Cartesian: box volume (straight XYZ travel)
Cylindrical: cylinder volume (rotate + extend + lift)
Spherical/polar: spherical sector volume (point + extend)
Cartesian is box-like.
Cylindrical is cylinder-like.
Spherical or polar is sector-like.
Articulated is irregular and flexible.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Waist rotation (Axis 1) — why it matters so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students often memorize manipulator names without connecting them to the shape of space they serve well.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Workspace shapes (intuition) first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture how robot geometry creates a characteristic workspace shape.
slides.scene_hint: Fade in box, cylinder, and spherical-shell volumes one at a time so students can connect manipulator geometry to workspace shape.

slides.narration_seed: Two robots might have the same reach, but if one has limited waist rotation, it may not cover the same stations in a cell.
must_cover: Waist Rotation: how many degrees the first joint of the robot can rotate
This dictates if the robot can reach behind itself (coverage around the base)
It strongly affects cell layout (where conveyors/fixtures/stations can be placed)
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Waist rotation (Axis 1) — why it matters to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Waist rotation (Axis 1) — why it matters is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Waist rotation (Axis 1) — why it matters in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Waist rotation (Axis 1) — why it matters connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Waist rotation (Axis 1) — why it matters accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Speed in a datasheet is not always your real speed. A heavy tool and fast acceleration demands will slow it down.
must_cover: Rotational Speeds: maximum speed a joint can rotate
TCP/tool speed depends on the path, acceleration, payload, and joint limits
Cycle time is what matters in production (speed specs help compare models, not predict exact cycle time)
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Rotational speeds (what to look at) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Rotational speeds (what to look at) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Rotational speeds (what to look at) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Rotational speeds (what to look at) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Rotational speeds (what to look at) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: If the robot is on a weak table, you’ll get vibration and poor results. Robot weight affects installation, safety, and performance.
must_cover: Weight: important to calculate mounting and estimating payload
Mounting location matters: floor, wall, ceiling, pedestal strength and bolts
Vibration affects accuracy and repeatability (rigid mounting improves results)
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Robot weight (practical reasons) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Robot weight (practical reasons) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Robot weight (practical reasons) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Robot weight (practical reasons) connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Robot weight (practical reasons) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: This is an important exam-style distinction. Slow down slightly.
must_cover: Accuracy: how close the robot gets to the desired position
Repeatability: when commanded to go to the same position multiple times, how much does the position of the end effector change
Precision: how accurately can a robot differentiate positions (e.g., 2.000 mm vs 2.001 mm)
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
Accuracy is closeness to target.
Repeatability is consistency when repeating the same move.
Precision is how finely position can be differentiated.
must_say: Repeatability is consistency. Accuracy is closeness to the intended target.
Repeatability means landing in the same place again and again. Accuracy means landing where you intended.
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from mechanical specifications into the quality vocabulary that students often mix together.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to prove that a robot can still be consistent while being wrong.
likely_student_confusion: Students often collapse accuracy, repeatability, and precision into one vague idea.
Students often use accuracy, repeatability, and precision interchangeably.
slides.explanation_style: Separate the terms carefully and anchor each one to what students would see physically on the shop floor.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should leave able to define all three terms without collapsing them together.

slide_ref.source_title: A robot can be repeatable but not accurate

slides.narration_seed: Use the 2 mm left example clearly and simply.
must_cover: Example: the robot always lands 2 mm left of the target point
That is low accuracy, but high repeatability (same error every time)
Calibration/offsets can improve accuracy; repeatability is mainly about mechanics + control stability
Same offset every time means good repeatability but poor accuracy.
Offsets and calibration can improve accuracy.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use A robot can be repeatable but not accurate to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once A robot can be repeatable but not accurate is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define A robot can be repeatable but not accurate in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep A robot can be repeatable but not accurate connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain A robot can be repeatable but not accurate accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slide_ref.source_title: What affects accuracy in real life?

slides.narration_seed: Even if the controller is perfect, real hardware bends and has small gaps. That’s why stiffness and gearbox quality matter.
must_cover: Stiffness and deflection under load (bending changes the tool position)
Backlash in gearboxes (lost motion during direction changes)
Calibration quality (model errors become position errors)
Temperature and wear over time (changes geometry and friction)
slides.transition_from_previous: Use What affects accuracy in real life? to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once What affects accuracy in real life? is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define What affects accuracy in real life? in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep What affects accuracy in real life? connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain What affects accuracy in real life? accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slide_ref.source_title: Manipulator types: how we classify them

slides.narration_seed: Use this as the transition from characteristics to robot types.
must_cover: A practical classification is by the type of joints used for the first three joints
The first three joints tend to control the overall placement of the end effector
Different joint choices create different motion styles and workspace shapes
Manipulator types are often classified by the first three joints.
Those joints strongly shape workspace and motion style.
must_say: Manipulator type is really a shortcut for workspace shape, motion freedom, and the tasks the robot handles best.
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from What affects accuracy in real life? into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
slides.explanation_style: Define Manipulator types: how we classify them in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Present the slide like a sorting decision: which family would you shortlist first for each kind of task?
slides.delivery_goal: Students should see manipulator type as geometry plus best-fit task, not just a name to memorize.
slides.scene_hint: Bring robot silhouettes onto the screen one by one and tie each family to its characteristic motion pattern and workspace.

slides.narration_seed: Cartesian robots are basically XYZ stages. They’re common in CNC, 3D printing, and precision moves in a rectangular space.
must_cover: Typical joint pattern: PPP (X-Y-Z)
Workspace: box volume
Advantages: stiff, simple equations to determine joint positions, very precise
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
Disadvantages: limited workspace for size (structure grows fast for large areas)
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from Manipulator types: how we classify them into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on Type 1: Cartesian (Gantry) explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of Type 1: Cartesian (Gantry) and know which side fits which situation.
slides.scene_hint: Use a simplified robot silhouette and workspace volume so students can see how geometry shapes where the robot can work.

slides.narration_seed: Explain Cartesian robots as XYZ systems with stiffness and precision advantages.
must_cover: CNC / laser cutting / plasma cutting (high stiffness is valuable)
Pick-and-place over a large flat area (simple XY coverage)
Machine tending with linear axes (straight approach into machines)
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
Cartesian usually means PPP motion.
Its workspace is box-shaped.
Its biggest strengths are stiffness and precision.
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from Type 1: Cartesian (Gantry) into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on Cartesian: where you see it most explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of Cartesian: where you see it most and know which side fits which situation.
slides.scene_hint: Use a simplified robot silhouette and workspace volume so students can see how geometry shapes where the robot can work.

slides.narration_seed: Cylindrical robots sweep around like a column. Simple and practical for certain handling tasks, but not as flexible as articulated arms.
must_cover: Typical joint pattern: RPP (rotate + radial + vertical)
Workspace: cylindrical volume
Advantages: very stiff in Z and along the length of the horizontal link; simple equations to determine joint positions
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
Disadvantages: limited workspace for size (less coverage compared to articulated for similar footprint)
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from Cartesian: where you see it most into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on Type 2: Cylindrical explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of Type 2: Cylindrical and know which side fits which situation.
slides.scene_hint: Use a simplified robot silhouette and workspace volume so students can see how geometry shapes where the robot can work.

slides.narration_seed: Polar robots point in a direction and extend. They can cover a large region, but their motion can be awkward around obstacles, so they’re less common today.
must_cover: Typical joint pattern: RRP (two rotations + extension)
Workspace: spherical sector / shell-like
Advantages: stiff in the direction of extension; larger workspace for size
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
Disadvantages: cannot go around obstacles; moderately complicated equations; uncommon in industry
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from Type 2: Cylindrical into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on Type 3: Spherical (Polar) explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of Type 3: Spherical (Polar) and know which side fits which situation.
slides.scene_hint: Use a simplified robot silhouette and workspace volume so students can see how geometry shapes where the robot can work.

slides.narration_seed: Describe cylindrical as rotate, extend, and lift.
must_cover: Typical pattern: RRP (often RRPR with wrist rotation)
Workspace: donut shape in XY + vertical Z
Advantages: stiff in the vertical direction; large workspace for size; capable of very rapid motion; lower cost over articulated robots
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
Typical pattern is RPP.
Workspace is cylindrical.
It is simpler but less flexible than articulated robots.
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from Type 3: Spherical (Polar) into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on Type 4: SCARA explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of Type 4: SCARA and know which side fits which situation.
slides.scene_hint: Use a simplified robot silhouette and workspace volume so students can see how geometry shapes where the robot can work.

slides.narration_seed: Keep this short and practical because it is less common.
must_cover: Joint pattern: usually RRRRRR (6 revolute joints)
Workspace: large workspace for size; able to reach around obstacles
Advantages: capable of rapid motion; most flexible for 3D tasks
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
Typical pattern is RRP.
Workspace is spherical or shell-like.
It is less common today partly because obstacle handling is limited.
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from Type 4: SCARA into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on Type 5: Articulated (6-axis industrial arm) explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of Type 5: Articulated (6-axis industrial arm) and know which side fits which situation.
slides.scene_hint: Use a simplified robot silhouette and workspace volume so students can see how geometry shapes where the robot can work.

slides.narration_seed: Live demo: move J1–J3 to see positioning, then J4–J6 to see wrist/orientation. Emphasize: axes are not all parallel—many are perpendicular, which enables full 3D orientation.
must_cover: State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
slides.transition_from_previous: Shift from static classification to the motion students need to actually see to understand.
slides.transition_to_next: Once students have seen the articulated arm move, connect that flexibility to the manipulator family comparison.
likely_student_confusion: Students may think URDF, Xacro, links, and joints are just file syntax instead of a robot-structure model.
Students may see six joints moving but still not understand what extra flexibility the wrist contributes.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on Interactive: 6-axis articulated joint motion (RRRRRR) explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to narrate the difference between large positioning joints and smaller orientation joints.
slides.scene_hint: Use the articulated arm model to step through what each joint contributes before zooming back out to the full pose flexibility.

slides.narration_seed: Make SCARA sound fast, table-focused, and vertically stiff.
must_cover: Parallel structure: multiple arms support one moving platform
Extremely fast pick-and-place for light payloads
Often mounted overhead above a conveyor (packaging/sorting)
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
SCARA is built for fast planar work and vertical insertion.
It is common in electronics assembly and packaging.
It is usually a better fit than a 6-axis arm for fast table assembly.
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from Interactive: 6-axis articulated joint motion (RRRRRR) into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on Type 6: Parallel robot (Delta) explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a real robot-cell decision where the team must justify a choice with specifications and task constraints.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of Type 6: Parallel robot (Delta) and know which side fits which situation.
slides.scene_hint: Use a simplified robot silhouette and workspace volume so students can see how geometry shapes where the robot can work.

slide_ref.source_title: One-slide memory: who is best at what?

slides.narration_seed: If you remember these five lines, you can explain 80% of robot selection logic. Everything else is basically a tradeoff between speed, stiffness, workspace, and flexibility.
must_cover: Cartesian: stiffness + precision in a box space
SCARA: fastest planar assembly + vertical insertion
Articulated: most flexible 3D motion (general-purpose)
Students should distinguish key datasheet terms such as payload, reach, workspace, accuracy, and repeatability.
Delta: fastest light pick-and-place
must_say: Do not memorize manipulator families in isolation. Link each one to workspace shape, strengths, and typical tasks.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use the recap to consolidate the manipulator comparison before the lesson ends.
slides.transition_to_next: Close by pointing students toward the simplest decision rule they should remember after class.
slides.explanation_style: Compress the lesson into a memorable decision table and speak like you are helping students build a quick exam-and-lab memory.
slides.story_hint: Keep One-slide memory: who is best at what? connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should leave with one quick mental map for matching robot types to tasks.

slides.narration_seed: Present articulated robots as the general-purpose industry default.
must_cover: Case 1: accurately cut a sheet of metal (forces on the robot can cause errors)
Case 2: spray paint large parts from various angles (exact positioning not important)
Give students a moment to decide before explaining the answer.
Articulated robots are usually six-axis revolute arms.
They are the most flexible general-purpose industrial robots.
Their tradeoff is lower stiffness and more complex kinematics than Cartesian systems.
slides.transition_from_previous: Turn the previous explanation into a decision on Practice: pick a robot type (and why) and let students test whether they can reason with the idea.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to generalize the reasoning students just practiced.
likely_student_confusion: Students may rush to the answer on Practice: pick a robot type (and why) without first stating the reasoning steps.
slides.explanation_style: Turn Practice: pick a robot type (and why) into a guided decision: pause for thought, then reveal the reasoning step by step.
slides.story_hint: Tell students to imagine they are reviewing a robot cell or lab task and need to defend their answer to a teammate.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to answer a similar Practice: pick a robot type (and why) question on their own and explain the reasoning.

slides.narration_seed: Treat this as a real demo slide. Be more energetic and guide attention carefully.
must_cover: DoF (can it do the motion you need?)
Payload (include tool + part + margin)
Reach + workspace (can it physically reach all points?)
J1 to J3 mainly place the arm in space.
J4 to J6 mainly control wrist orientation.
Not all axes are parallel, which gives full 3D orientation capability.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use What I want you to check in a datasheet to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once What I want you to check in a datasheet is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define What I want you to check in a datasheet in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep What I want you to check in a datasheet connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain What I want you to check in a datasheet accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Present Delta robots as speed champions for light payload pick-and-place.
must_cover: In one sentence: difference between reach and workspace?
Accuracy vs repeatability: which is usually better in industrial robots?
Name one task where SCARA is better than a 6-axis arm
Delta robots are extremely fast for light payloads.
They are common above conveyors in packaging and sorting.
Their tradeoff is smaller workspace and lower heavy-payload capability.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Exit questions to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Close by restating the destination of the topic: Students should finish able to explain both what the main specs mean and which manipulator type fits a given task.
slides.explanation_style: Define Exit questions in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Exit questions connected to what an industrial robotics student would actually need in lab or robot selection.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Exit questions accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.
